title: The Manhattan Project Rebuttal: Why Closed Source Doesn't Actually Protect Anything

source.original: On Tech Politics/Policy — Marc Andreessen & Ben Horowitz (a16z, April 2024)

body_html:
The Manhattan Project analogy is the most popular argument for closed-source AI. Vinod Khosla deploys it. Sam Altman has flirted with it. The structure is always the same: AI is a transformative, dangerous technology, therefore — like the original atomic bomb program — it must be developed in secret, by trusted actors, with the source kept under wraps. Andreessen's rebuttal in the tech politics video is short, brutal, and almost impossible to recover from once you've heard it.

The real Manhattan Project was a security operation. Los Alamos was a literal city in the desert that didn't appear on maps. Every employee was vetted and surveilled. Foreign nationals were excluded with extreme prejudice. The compartmentalization was so aggressive that most workers didn't know what they were building. Communication out was monitored, censored, or banned. Physical access was controlled by armed soldiers. The bomb design itself was protected with the kind of paranoia that only wartime can justify.

Now look at OpenAI. Or Google. Or Anthropic. Or any of the labs Khosla and his allies want to designate as the "trusted few" who get to develop frontier AI. These are open-plan San Francisco offices with badge access and a handful of HR policies. They employ thousands of people, including many foreign nationals from countries the US considers adversarial. They use cloud infrastructure with internet-facing services. They publish papers, give talks, post code samples on GitHub. Their security practices are roughly equivalent to a mid-tier fintech company. The idea that any of these labs could keep frontier model weights actually secret in any meaningful adversarial sense is, in Andreessen's words, laughable.

The kicker landed three days after the recorded conversation: the Justice Department indicted a Chinese national Google employee who had downloaded Google's next-generation AI chip designs — the equivalent of stealing the bomb's blueprints — and walked them to China. According to the indictment, Google didn't notice that the engineer had been physically in China for six months. Google. The company that has more security infrastructure than most governments. Six months. Of an active employee with access to the family jewels of their AI program, sitting in Beijing.

This is the actual security posture of closed-source AI. The horse has not just left the barn — it left the barn, ran across the country, and is now grazing in a different barn. The closed-source argument doesn't fail at the philosophical level. It fails at the operational level. There is no realistic configuration of these companies under which their model weights stay inside the building, because the building is full of doors, the doors are full of people, and the people are full of incentives that no badge system can fix.

And here's the second twist: even if you could keep the weights secret, the math itself is in published academic papers. The transformer architecture is in a 2017 Google paper. RLHF is in dozens of public papers. Mixture-of-experts, attention mechanisms, scaling laws — all open. Anyone with a PhD-level understanding of ML can replicate the techniques given enough compute. The only thing closed-source actually protects is the specific trained weights of a specific company's model — and those leak too, repeatedly, from every lab.

So what does "closed-source AI for safety" actually buy you? Not security against China — China already has the techniques and is actively recruiting the people. Not security against rogue actors — the math is public. Not security against future leaks — the leaks have been happening for years. The only thing it buys is protection from open-source competitors who would otherwise eat the labs' margins. The Manhattan Project frame is rhetoric. The actual function is monopoly maintenance.

When Andreessen pressed Khosla on this in the Stanford congressional meeting, Khosla's fallback was telling: he pivoted to the cost argument — "it costs billions to train, why give it away?" That isn't a safety argument. That's an investor protecting his portfolio. Both arguments can't be the real one. The cost argument is the real one. The safety frame is just the wrapper that makes it sellable in Washington.
